texts.orig: Preparation of AlOOH/Al2O3 porous ceramics having CO2/N2 gas selectivity of less than 1
texts.text: Preparation of AlOOH/Al2O3 porous ceramics having CO2/N2 gas selectivity of less than 1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Porous Al2O3 bodies, having an average pore size of approximately 74 nm and a porosity of 40.4%, were prepared by the slip-cast method. The intended porous ceramics were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of the porous Al2O3 bodies. The obtained samples consisted of AlOOH and α -Al2O3 phases, and the α -Al2O3 surfaces were coated with AlOOH particles. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples depended on the conditions of the hydrothermal treatment and were found to decrease with increasing hydrothermal treatment time. However, the gas selectivities of the samples heated for 96 h were nearly equivalent to those of the samples heated for 72 h. A high concentration of Al(NO3)3, which was used as the aluminum source, leads to a decreased CO2/N2 gas selectivity. Moreover, CO2/N2 gas selectivity decreased slightly at low pressure drop. The minimum selectivity attained was approximately 0.69 at 0.02 MPa. & 2015 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
texts.text: Porous Al2O3 bodies, having an average pore size of approximately 74 nm and a porosity of 40.4%, were prepared by the slip-cast method. The intended porous ceramics were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of the porous Al2O3 bodies. The obtained samples consisted of AlOOH and α -Al2O3 phases, and the α -Al2O3 surfaces were coated with AlOOH particles. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples depended on the conditions of the hydrothermal treatment and were found to decrease with increasing hydrothermal treatment time. However, the gas selectivities of the samples heated for 96 h were nearly equivalent to those of the samples heated for 72 h. A high concentration of Al(NO3)3, which was used as the aluminum source, leads to a decreased CO2/N2 gas selectivity. Moreover, CO2/N2 gas selectivity decreased slightly at low pressure drop. The minimum selectivity attained was approximately 0.69 at 0.02 MPa. & 2015 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Carbon dioxide capture and storage (CCS), which captures CO2 from large-scale discharge sources, such as factories and electric power plants, and con fi nes it underground or underwater, is an interesting method for reducing apparent CO2 gas emissions substantially and rapidly [1]. The CCS consists of four stages: separation and capture of CO2 and its subsequent transportation, injection, and sequestration. Because separation and capture are considered to accounting for approximately 60% of total CCS costs, establishing low-cost separation and capture technology is the most important issue related to the practical application of CCS [2]. Because the membrane separation method using ceramic membranes enables low-cost, requires no maintenance, and allows continuous processing, numerous membrane materials have been reported such as zeolite [3 -6], silica [7 -9], alumina [10], and carbon [11 -13]. These membranes demonstrate excellent gas selectivity and gas separation abilities. However, not all the
texts.text: Carbon dioxide capture and storage (CCS), which captures CO2 from large-scale discharge sources, such as factories and electric power plants, and con fi nes it underground or underwater, is an interesting method for reducing apparent CO2 gas emissions substantially and rapidly [1]. The CCS consists of four stages: separation and capture of CO2 and its subsequent transportation, injection, and sequestration. Because separation and capture are considered to accounting for approximately 60% of total CCS costs, establishing low-cost separation and capture technology is the most important issue related to the practical application of CCS [2]. Because the membrane separation method using ceramic membranes enables low-cost, requires no maintenance, and allows continuous processing, numerous membrane materials have been reported such as zeolite [3 -6], silica [7 -9], alumina [10], and carbon [11 -13]. These membranes demonstrate excellent gas selectivity and gas separation abilities. However, not all the

texts.label: text
texts.orig: reported membranes have attained a reasonable tradeoff between selectivity and permeability ostensibly dictated by Robeson's upper bound [14 -15]. A common solution to this de fi ciency is to increase the gas permeability coef fi cient by thinning the separation membrane.
texts.text: reported membranes have attained a reasonable tradeoff between selectivity and permeability ostensibly dictated by Robeson's upper bound [14 -15]. A common solution to this de fi ciency is to increase the gas permeability coef fi cient by thinning the separation membrane.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As an alternative solution to matters related to the gas permeability coef fi cient, porous ceramic fi lters have been suggested [16]. This previous study particularly focused on the control of the pore size of porous ceramic fi lters and the pressure drop during gas permeation testing. Therefore, the pore size was fi rst adjusted to a size equivalent to the mean free path of the gas molecules to be selected by the membrane. Then, the pore walls of the porous ceramic fi lters were coated with AlOOH. After investigating the pressure dependence of the gas selectivity, it was established that the N2/CO2 gas selectivity increased at a lower pressure drop. It was suggested that these results originate from the difference between the N2 and CO2 af fi nities with the pore walls, and N2/CO2 gas selectivity was improved for pore walls having a higher CO2 af fi nity. This fi lter was also advantageous in that the pore walls were modi fi ed with an inorganic substance, which has the advantage of high temperature durability. In fact, AlOOH used
texts.text: As an alternative solution to matters related to the gas permeability coef fi cient, porous ceramic fi lters have been suggested [16]. This previous study particularly focused on the control of the pore size of porous ceramic fi lters and the pressure drop during gas permeation testing. Therefore, the pore size was fi rst adjusted to a size equivalent to the mean free path of the gas molecules to be selected by the membrane. Then, the pore walls of the porous ceramic fi lters were coated with AlOOH. After investigating the pressure dependence of the gas selectivity, it was established that the N2/CO2 gas selectivity increased at a lower pressure drop. It was suggested that these results originate from the difference between the N2 and CO2 af fi nities with the pore walls, and N2/CO2 gas selectivity was improved for pore walls having a higher CO2 af fi nity. This fi lter was also advantageous in that the pore walls were modi fi ed with an inorganic substance, which has the advantage of high temperature durability. In fact, AlOOH used

texts.text: in a previous study was stable at 500 1 C, which is approximately 300 1 C higher than a typical organic modi fi cation such as a silane coupling agent [7,8]. Therefore, this membrane can be expected to be applicable to a wide temperature range.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This previous study is also signi fi cant from the standpoint of preparing the membrane with an N2/CO2 gas selectivity of greater than 1 (a CO2/N2 gas selectivity less than 1). Robeson has reported a membrane with a He/CO2 or H2/CO2 gas selectivity of both, greater as well as less than 1. However, a polymer membrane with a CO2/N2 selectivity of less than 1 has not been reported [14,15]. In this respect, it is considered that porous ceramics with greater gas selectivity is positioned as a new membrane. In the present study, serving as a second stage of a series of research, we investigate the relation between AlOOH synthesis conditions, which are yet to be well considered.
texts.text: This previous study is also signi fi cant from the standpoint of preparing the membrane with an N2/CO2 gas selectivity of greater than 1 (a CO2/N2 gas selectivity less than 1). Robeson has reported a membrane with a He/CO2 or H2/CO2 gas selectivity of both, greater as well as less than 1. However, a polymer membrane with a CO2/N2 selectivity of less than 1 has not been reported [14,15]. In this respect, it is considered that porous ceramics with greater gas selectivity is positioned as a new membrane. In the present study, serving as a second stage of a series of research, we investigate the relation between AlOOH synthesis conditions, which are yet to be well considered.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The preparation method for AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics is approximately the same as that employed previously [16]. The porous Al2O3 bodies were prepared by combining highpurity α -Al2O3 (TM-DAR; Taimei Chemicals Co. Ltd., Nagano, Japan) with 70 vol% distilled water and 0.62 mass% ammonium poly(acrylic acid) (Celuna D-305; Chukyo Yushi Co. Ltd., Aichi, Japan) as a dispersant. Mixing was accomplished using a planetary-type mixer [17,18] with a stainless steel mesh for 2 min, rotated at 800 rpm and revolved at 2000 rpm. The α -Al2O3 loading for the slurry was adjusted to 30 vol%. The obtained slurries were then slip-cast into 16 mm φ gypsum molds for 5 min, dried at room temperature for 24 h, and then heated at 1000 1 C for 2 h. The obtained Al2O3 bodies were soaked in a 0.5 -2 mol/L Al(NO3)3 (Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd., Osaka, Japan) aqueous solution in a vacuum chamber for 12 h to insert Al atoms into the pore spaces. The obtained samples were subsequently heated in a Te fl on-lined autoclave with a 1 mol/L NH3 aqueous solution at 200 1 C for 48 -96 h. After hydrothermal treatments, all samples were dried at 90 1 C for 1 day. The density and porosity of the obtained samples were measured using Archimedes' technique with water. The average pore size of the samples was measured using mercury intrusion porosimetry (Pascal 240; CE Instruments Ltd., Italy). Their microstructures were observed using a fi eld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, S4500; Hitachi High-Technologies Corp., Tokyo, Japan). The phases formed in the samples were identi fi ed using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with CuK α radiation (LabX XRD-6100; Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan). Carbon dioxide ( m CO2) and nitrogen ( m N2) permeability coef fi cients were measured at room temperature using the following equation in conjunction with gas permeametry equipment [19]:
texts.text: The preparation method for AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics is approximately the same as that employed previously [16]. The porous Al2O3 bodies were prepared by combining highpurity α -Al2O3 (TM-DAR; Taimei Chemicals Co. Ltd., Nagano, Japan) with 70 vol% distilled water and 0.62 mass% ammonium poly(acrylic acid) (Celuna D-305; Chukyo Yushi Co. Ltd., Aichi, Japan) as a dispersant. Mixing was accomplished using a planetary-type mixer [17,18] with a stainless steel mesh for 2 min, rotated at 800 rpm and revolved at 2000 rpm. The α -Al2O3 loading for the slurry was adjusted to 30 vol%. The obtained slurries were then slip-cast into 16 mm φ gypsum molds for 5 min, dried at room temperature for 24 h, and then heated at 1000 1 C for 2 h. The obtained Al2O3 bodies were soaked in a 0.5 -2 mol/L Al(NO3)3 (Wako Pure Chemical Industries Ltd., Osaka, Japan) aqueous solution in a vacuum chamber for 12 h to insert Al atoms into the pore spaces. The obtained samples were subsequently heated in a Te fl on-lined autoclave with a 1 mol/L NH3 aqueous solution at 200 1 C for 48 -96 h. After hydrothermal treatments, all samples were dried at 90 1 C for 1 day. The density and porosity of the obtained samples were measured using Archimedes' technique with water. The average pore size of the samples was measured using mercury intrusion porosimetry (Pascal 240; CE Instruments Ltd., Italy). Their microstructures were observed using a fi eld emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, S4500; Hitachi High-Technologies Corp., Tokyo, Japan). The phases formed in the samples were identi fi ed using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with CuK α radiation (LabX XRD-6100; Shimadzu Corp., Kyoto, Japan). Carbon dioxide ( m CO2) and nitrogen ( m N2) permeability coef fi cients were measured at room temperature using the following equation in conjunction with gas permeametry equipment [19]:

Ltd., Tokyo, Japan), Q is the fl ow rate measured using a mass fl ow meter (Model8500; Kojima Instruments Inc., Kyoto, Japan), and A and L denote the cross sectional area and the fi lter thickness, respectively. texts.orig: The speci fi c surface area ( S BET) was measured by the Brunauer -Emmett -Teller (BET) method using N2 gas in a high-precision surface area and pore size analyzer (Belsorp mini II; Bel Japan Inc., Osaka, Japan). CO2 adsorption -desorption isotherms were measured at � 79 1 C in the Belsorp mini II to estimate CO2 gas af fi nity for the samples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this subsection, all samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment were prepared with a 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution. The XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 1, wherein the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment consists of only α -Al2O3. The sample subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 48 h exhibits an extremely small peak at approximately 14.3 1 (denoted by the fi lled circle in the fi gure), which is identi fi ed as AlOOH. This peak is clearly evident in samples subjected to extended periods of hydrothermal treatment. The XRD data indicate that Al(NO3)3 converted to AlOOH during hydrothermal treatment. It should be noted that the two peaks at 38 1 and 43 1 are identi fi ed as the sample holder (marked by open squares in Fig. 1).
texts.text: In this subsection, all samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment were prepared with a 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution. The XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 1, wherein the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment consists of only α -Al2O3. The sample subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 48 h exhibits an extremely small peak at approximately 14.3 1 (denoted by the fi lled circle in the fi gure), which is identi fi ed as AlOOH. This peak is clearly evident in samples subjected to extended periods of hydrothermal treatment. The XRD data indicate that Al(NO3)3 converted to AlOOH during hydrothermal treatment. It should be noted that the two peaks at 38 1 and 43 1 are identi fi ed as the sample holder (marked by open squares in Fig. 1).

On the other hand, the Al2O3 surface is covered with nanometer-sized crystals after hydrothermal treatment. In consideration for the XRD results, it is assumed that these crystals consist of the AlOOH phase. The preparation

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The N2 and CO2 gas permeability coef fi cients and the CO2/N2 selectivities of the samples were calculated from Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively. The obtained results are plotted with respect to the pressure drop (MPa), as shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the N2 gas permeability prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 1.27 -10 � 9 kmol m m � 2 s � 1 kPa � 1 , which was nearly constant over the measurement range, and the N2 gas permeability is observed to decrease with increasing hydrothermal treatment time. It is considered that AlOOH may have precipitated in the pores and inhibited gas permeation. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the CO2 permeability of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 1.00 -10 � 9 kmol m m � 2 s � 1 kPa � 1 at a pressure drop of 0.02 MPa. The CO2 permeability very slightly increased with increasing pressure drop. However, this permeability change became increasingly signi fi cant as hydrothermal treatment time increased, and the variation was clari fi ed from consideration of the CO2/N2 gas selectivity, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The gas selectivity of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment was nearly constant at 0.8 for a pressure drop greater than 0.04 MPa, and decreased slightly at a lower pressure drop. The constant value exhibited by the untreated sample corresponds to the gas selectivity characterized by Knudsen diffusion, calculated from following Eq. using the respective molecular weights of CO2 ( M CO2) and N2 ( M N2) [20]:

The obtained results are plotted with respect to the pressure drop (MPa), as shown in Fig. 3. As shown in Fig. 3(a), the N2 gas permeability prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 1.27 -10 � 9 kmol m m � 2 s � 1 kPa � 1 , which was nearly constant over the measurement range, and the N2 gas permeability is observed to decrease with increasing hydrothermal treatment time. It is considered that AlOOH may have precipitated in the pores and inhibited gas permeation. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the CO2 permeability of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 1.00 -10 � 9 kmol m m � 2 s � 1 kPa � 1 at a pressure drop of 0.02 MPa. The CO2 permeability very slightly increased with increasing pressure drop. However, this permeability change became increasingly signi fi cant as hydrothermal treatment time increased, and the variation was clari fi ed from consideration of the CO2/N2 gas selectivity, as shown in Fig. 3(c). The gas selectivity of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment was nearly constant at 0.8 for a pressure drop greater than 0.04 MPa, and decreased slightly at a lower pressure drop. The constant value exhibited by the untreated sample corresponds to the gas selectivity characterized by Knudsen diffusion, calculated from following Eq. using the respective molecular weights of CO2 ( M CO2) and N2 ( M N2) [20]:

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The gas selectivity was moderately enhanced after hydrothermal treatment for 48 h and was further emphasized after hydrothermal treatment for 72 h, where the gas selectivity of the sample treated for 96 h was nearly identical to that treated for 72 h. The XRD, microstructural, and gas permeability data suggest that the extent of AlOOH crystal coverage increases when the hydrothermal treatment is conducted for 96 h; however, little or no effect on the gas selectivity is observed. Therefore, it appears that hydrothermal treatment for 72 h represents an optimum improvement in the gas selectivity.
texts.text: The gas selectivity was moderately enhanced after hydrothermal treatment for 48 h and was further emphasized after hydrothermal treatment for 72 h, where the gas selectivity of the sample treated for 96 h was nearly identical to that treated for 72 h. The XRD, microstructural, and gas permeability data suggest that the extent of AlOOH crystal coverage increases when the hydrothermal treatment is conducted for 96 h; however, little or no effect on the gas selectivity is observed. Therefore, it appears that hydrothermal treatment for 72 h represents an optimum improvement in the gas selectivity.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The porosity of the AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 40.4%. The samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 72 h indicate that increasing the Al(NO3)3 concentration from 0.5 to 1 mol/L decreased the porosity from 39.2% to 38.9%, as shown in Table 1. To evaluate this change carefully, the XRD patterns, microstructures, and pore size distributions of samples prepared with 0.5, 1, and 2 (to a minor extent) mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solutions and subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 72 h were investigated. First, the XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 4. The sample prior to hydrothermal treatment consists of only α -Al2O3. The sample prepared with the 0.5 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution exhibits an extremely small AlOOH peak (marked by the closed circle in the fi gure), which is clearly evident for the sample prepared with the 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution. Both samples show only peaks associated with the α -Al2O3 and AlOOH phases.
texts.text: The porosity of the AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics prior to hydrothermal treatment was approximately 40.4%. The samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 72 h indicate that increasing the Al(NO3)3 concentration from 0.5 to 1 mol/L decreased the porosity from 39.2% to 38.9%, as shown in Table 1. To evaluate this change carefully, the XRD patterns, microstructures, and pore size distributions of samples prepared with 0.5, 1, and 2 (to a minor extent) mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solutions and subjected to hydrothermal treatment for 72 h were investigated. First, the XRD patterns of the samples are shown in Fig. 4. The sample prior to hydrothermal treatment consists of only α -Al2O3. The sample prepared with the 0.5 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution exhibits an extremely small AlOOH peak (marked by the closed circle in the fi gure), which is clearly evident for the sample prepared with the 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution. Both samples show only peaks associated with the α -Al2O3 and AlOOH phases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: was conducted, and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 6. Identical to the treated samples, the AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics prior to hydrothermal treatment exhibit a low pore volume at pore sizes greater than 100 nm. The pore volume increases very steeply at approximately 80 nm and is nearly constant at pore sizes less than approximately 40 nm. The total pore volume and the average pore size were approximately 167 mm 3 /g and 74 nm, respectively. On the other hand, the curves of the samples after hydrothermal treatment exhibit a slightly different tendency. The samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment demonstrate an increasing pore volume at a pore size of approximately 60 nm, which is smaller than that of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment. However, the slope of the sample prepared with the 0.5 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution levels off at approximately 40 nm, whereas the sample prepared with the 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution levels off at less than 30 nm and exhibits a consistently smaller pore volume.
texts.text: was conducted, and the obtained results are shown in Fig. 6. Identical to the treated samples, the AlOOH/Al2O3 ceramics prior to hydrothermal treatment exhibit a low pore volume at pore sizes greater than 100 nm. The pore volume increases very steeply at approximately 80 nm and is nearly constant at pore sizes less than approximately 40 nm. The total pore volume and the average pore size were approximately 167 mm 3 /g and 74 nm, respectively. On the other hand, the curves of the samples after hydrothermal treatment exhibit a slightly different tendency. The samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment demonstrate an increasing pore volume at a pore size of approximately 60 nm, which is smaller than that of the sample prior to hydrothermal treatment. However, the slope of the sample prepared with the 0.5 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution levels off at approximately 40 nm, whereas the sample prepared with the 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution levels off at less than 30 nm and exhibits a consistently smaller pore volume.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: These results indicate that the pore size distribution was altered by the amount of precipitated AlOOH. In addition, the porosity of the sample prepared with the 2 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution was approximately the same as that of the sample prepared with the 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution. It is considered that no signi fi cant difference between these two samples can be observed other than the fact that the higher concentration Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution resulted in damaged samples due to a slight difference in the amount of AlOOH precipitation. As a result, 1 mol/L is thought to be the optimum Al(NO3)3 concentration for this study.

texts.orig: The N2 and CO2 gas permeability coef fi cients and the CO2/ N2 selectivities of these samples were calculated from Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively, and the results are shown in Fig. 7. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the N2 gas permeabilities of the samples prepared with 0.5 and 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solutions were approximately 1.0 -10 � 9 and 8.5 -10 � 10 kmol m m � 2 s � 1 kPa � 1 , respectively, independent of the pressure drop. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the CO2 gas permeabilities of these samples were lower than their N2 gas permeabilities, both of which slightly fell at a reduced pressure drop. The gas selectivities are shown in Fig. 7(c). The gas selectivities of the samples after hydrothermal treatment with the 0.5 and 1 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solutions were both lower than that of the sample prior to treatment over the entire pressure drop

texts.orig: range measured. The tendency for the gas selectivity to decrease at a low pressure drop was prominent in samples after hydrothermal treatment. It is regarded that this enhancement of gas selectivity originates from differences in the interaction of N2 and CO2 molecules with the pore walls. Knudsen diffusion occurs when the pore size is comparable to or smaller than the mean free path of the gas molecule. As mentioned in relation to Eq. (3), the gas selectivity is obtained from the square root of the molecular weight ratio if there is no interaction between the pore walls and the gas molecules. On the other hand, if there is no interaction between the pore walls and the gas molecules, the gas selectivity can be emphasized. In a previous paper, the interaction energy between AlOOH and CO2 was found to be larger than that between Al2O3 and CO2 [16].

texts.orig: � 79 1 C. Generally, the adsorption volume per weight is estimated. However, the samples before and after hydrothermal treatment have different S BET as well as chemical properties. Therefore, the CO2 adsorption volume was normalized by the S BET. This represents the difference of af fi nity for each gas for the samples because the S BET was calculated from N2 adsorption results. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 8. The CO2 adsorption volume of both samples increased with increasing P / P 0. Both adsorption curves were in good agreement with the desorption curves. The CO2 adsorption volume per surface area of the samples after hydrothermal treatment was larger than that of the samples before hydrothermal treatment. A high af fi nity between CO2 molecules and AlOOH is indicated. CO2 molecules are considered to diffuse more slowly through the pores coated with AlOOH. The effect of the interaction was observed to be very signi fi cant at a reduced pressure drop, although it was hardly observed at a high pressure drop. These results are a favorable factor when the present materials are used as a membrane support. When a gas separation membrane is formed on this membrane support, and the gas passes from the membrane side to the membrane support side, the gas pressure is reduced in the separation membrane, and the

texts.orig: pressure-drop through the membrane support is correspondingly decreased. Therefore, it is more effective that the gas selectivity of a membrane support was enhanced at a lower pressure drop than at a higher one. Thus, we have successfully prepared porous ceramics with a CO2/N2 gas selectivity of less than 1 by coating the pore walls with AlOOH, which has an emphasized af fi nity for CO2. In addition, gas selectivity is enhanced by increasing the AlOOH crystal coverage.

Thus, we have successfully prepared porous ceramics with a CO2/N2 gas selectivity of less than 1 by coating the pore walls with AlOOH, which has an emphasized af fi nity for CO2. In addition, gas selectivity is enhanced by increasing the AlOOH crystal coverage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Porous Al2O3 bodies, having an average pore size of approximately 74 nm and a porosity of 40.4%, were prepared by the slipcast method. The Al2O3 bodies were soaked in an Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution in a vacuum chamber to insert Al atoms into the pores. After hydrothermal treatment, the fi nal products were obtained. The samples consisted of AlOOH and α -Al2O3 phases, as determined by XRD. The α -Al2O3 surfaces were coated with AlOOH particles of diameters less than 20 nm, as observed by FESEM. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples decreased with increasing hydrothermal treatment time up to 72 h, and no appreciable emphasis was observed at longer treatment times. Utilization of a high concentration Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution leads to high CO2/N2 gas selectivity. However, the samples prepared with the 2 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution were damaged. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment were observed to decrease at low pressure drop. The minimum CO2/N2 gas selectivity was approximately 0.69 at a pressure drop of 0.02 MPa.
texts.text: Porous Al2O3 bodies, having an average pore size of approximately 74 nm and a porosity of 40.4%, were prepared by the slipcast method. The Al2O3 bodies were soaked in an Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution in a vacuum chamber to insert Al atoms into the pores. After hydrothermal treatment, the fi nal products were obtained. The samples consisted of AlOOH and α -Al2O3 phases, as determined by XRD. The α -Al2O3 surfaces were coated with AlOOH particles of diameters less than 20 nm, as observed by FESEM. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples decreased with increasing hydrothermal treatment time up to 72 h, and no appreciable emphasis was observed at longer treatment times. Utilization of a high concentration Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution leads to high CO2/N2 gas selectivity. However, the samples prepared with the 2 mol/L Al(NO3)3 aqueous solution were damaged. The CO2/N2 gas selectivities of the samples subjected to hydrothermal treatment were observed to decrease at low pressure drop. The minimum CO2/N2 gas selectivity was approximately 0.69 at a pressure drop of 0.02 MPa.
